Data Cleansing &
Transformation in Power BI

A complete step-by-step practical guide to cleaning raw procurement data using Power Query. Follow along and tick off each step as you work in Power BI.

Table of Contents

4.1Unzip the Source File4.2Connect to Data in Power BI Desktop4.3Remove Top Rows4.4Promote Header4.5Remove Column4.6Change Data Type4.7Replace Null for Numbers4.8Transform: Trim, Upper, Lower4.9Unpivot Columns4.10Pivot, Rename & Change Type4.11Filter Rows4.12Append Queries (Combine Datasets)4.13Load to Power BI4.14Power Query: Excel ↔ Power BI

Step-by-Step Instructions

4.1 Setup

Unzip the Source File

Extract the training files to a known Desktop location before connecting Power BI to your data.

1. Locate Power BI Training.zip → Right-click → Extract All → choose your Desktop
2. Navigate to Source File → Day2 — confirm you see SpendingDataset1 and SpendingDataset2
4.2 Setup

Connect to Data in Power BI Desktop

Link Power BI to your Excel source so you can begin transformation in Power Query.

1. 4.2.1 Open Power BI Desktop → choose Blank report
4.2.2 Click Home → Get Data → Excel workbook
2. 4.2.3 Navigate to SpendingDataset1 on the Desktop → click Open
4.2.4 In the Navigator, select Data on the left pane — preview appears on the right → click Transform Data
💡Always choose Transform Data rather than Load so you can clean the data first.
4.3 Cleaning

Remove Top Rows

The raw file contains metadata rows (e.g. 'Oman Energy Procurement Data', date range) that must be stripped before analysis.

1. 4.3.1 In Power Query Editor, select the Data query → go to Home → Remove Rows → Remove Top Rows
4.3.2 Enter 2 in Number of rows → click OK
4.4 Cleaning

Promote Header

After removing 2 rows, the actual column names (Material, Qty_Jan 2023…) are now in row 1 — promote them as column headers.

1. Go to Home → Use First Row as Headers
Verify column names like Material, Project, Qty_Jan 2023 now appear in the header bar
4.5 Cleaning

Remove Column

Irrelevant columns like 'notes' add noise and slow performance — remove them to keep the dataset lean.

1. Click the notes column header to select it → go to Home → Remove Columns (or right-click → Remove)
4.6 Cleaning

Change Data Type

Correct types enable proper calculations — summing a 'Text' price column returns errors instead of totals.

1. 4.6.1 Hold Ctrl and select all Qty_ and Price_ columns → go to Home → Data Type → Decimal Number
4.6.2 Select the Material and Project columns → go to Home → Data Type → Text
💡If prompted 'Replace current conversion?', choose Replace.
4.7 Cleaning

Replace Null for Numbers

Null values in numeric columns cause SUM/AVERAGE measures to return errors. Replacing with 0 keeps calculations safe.

1. 4.7.1 Select all numeric columns (Qty_ and Price_ columns) → go to Transform → Replace Values
4.7.2 Enter null in Value To Find → enter 0 in Replace With → click OK
💡The word null is case-sensitive in Power Query — use lowercase.
4.8 Cleaning

Transform: Trim, Upper, Lower

Inconsistent text like ' Oil ' (spaces) or 'gas' vs 'GAS' creates duplicate groupings in charts. Trim + Uppercase fixes this.

1. 4.8.1 Right-click the Material column → Transform → Trim (removes leading/trailing spaces)
4.8.2 Right-click the Material column again → Transform → UPPERCASE
💡Use Lowercase for email addresses, Uppercase for category codes.
4.9 Transform

Unpivot Columns

Wide columns (Qty_Jan 2023 … Price_Dec 2024) are hard to chart. Unpivoting converts them to rows — drag 3 columns instead of 24.

1. 4.9.1 Hold Ctrl and select Material and Project columns only → go to Transform → Unpivot Columns → Unpivot Other Columns
4.9.2 Right-click the Attribute column → Split Column → By Delimiter
2. 4.9.3 Choose Custom delimiter → enter _ → click OK
💡After splitting, Attribute.1 = type (Qty/Price), Attribute.2 = date period.
4.10 Transform

Pivot, Rename & Change Type

Re-pivot so Qty and Price each get their own column, then label and type the Date column correctly for time intelligence.

1. 4.10.1 Select Attribute.1 column → go to Transform → Pivot Column
4.10.2 Set Values Column to Value, Aggregate: Don't Aggregate → click OK
2. 4.10.3 Double-click Attribute.2 column → rename it to Date → press Tab
4.10.4 Select the Date column → Home → Data Type → Date
💡Always set Date type explicitly — Power BI time intelligence DAX functions require it.
4.11 Transform

Filter Rows

Zero or invalid quantity rows skew averages and pollute trend charts. Filter them out to keep only meaningful records.

1. 4.11.1 Click the dropdown arrow ▼ on the Qty column header
4.11.2 Uncheck 0 to exclude zero-quantity rows → click OK
4.12 Combine

Append Queries (Combine Datasets)

SpendingDataset2 contains additional procurement records. Appending stacks its rows under Dataset1 to create one complete table.

1. 4.12.1 Right-click the Data query → click Duplicate
4.12.2 Select Data (2) → in Properties rename it to Additional Data → press Tab
2. 4.12.3 In APPLIED STEPS click the ⚙ gear icon next to Source
4.12.4 Click Browse → navigate to SpendingDataset2 → click Open
4.12.5 Click OK to confirm the new source path
3. 4.12.6 Click OK to confirm
4.12.7 Right-click Additional Data query → click Enable Load
4.12.8 Select the main Data query → go to Home → Append Queries
4. 4.12.9 Select Additional Data from the dropdown → click OK
💡Disable load on Additional Data if you don't want it as a separate table — it only serves as an append source.
4.13 Load

Load to Power BI

All transformations are complete. Loading pushes the clean, unified dataset into Power BI for visualisation and report building.

1. Click Close & Apply in the Home ribbon of Power Query Editor
Wait for the loading bar to complete, then confirm the Data table appears in the Fields pane
💡If you see errors, click View Errors to trace which transformation step caused the issue.
4.14 Bonus

Power Query: Excel ↔ Power BI

Power Query uses the same M language engine in both tools. Queries built in Power BI can be copied directly into Excel — no rework needed.

1. Step 1 In Power BI: Home → Transform Data to reopen Power Query Editor
2. Step 2 Right-click the Data query → click Copy
Step 3 Open a blank Excel Workbook → go to Data → Queries & Connections
3. Step 4 In the Queries & Connections pane → right-click in blank space → Paste
4. Step 5 The query now exists in Excel — double-click it to open the identical Power Query interface
Step 6 All transformation steps are preserved and editable
💡Update file paths after pasting — they will still reference the original Power BI source location.

Module 4 Recap

You have completed the full data cleansing pipeline — from raw, messy Excel files to a clean, typed, unified table ready for Power BI reporting and DAX calculations.

The same Power Query steps work identically in Microsoft Excel. Copy queries between tools freely — prototype in Excel, scale in Power BI.

Power Query uses the M language under the hood in both tools — one skill, two platforms.

🎉 All 14 sections completed! Your data is clean and ready for Power BI reporting.
